reasoning_summary: Diagnosing live run 20260421_160557_bf16_gemm_v1_35400d35 (median runtime 24.691 ms, accepted base 24.195 ms, target <= 18 ms, cuBLAS ~22 ms). Round 10 (PTX wait-group collapse) improved barrier (10.16 -> 6.34) and short_scoreboard (6.74 -> 1.86) but paid for it with a 34-register jump (168 -> 202 regs/thread). That pushed launch__occupancy_limit_registers from 3 down to 2, theoretical occupancy from ~25% to 16.67%, and achieved active warps from ~24.7% to 16.58%. With only 4 warps/SM resident, long_scoreboard and mio_throttle regressed (2.27 -> 5.53 and 1.77 -> 4.0), and DRAM throughput slipped to 12.5%. The top bottleneck class is now occupancy_latency_hiding_issue at severity 43.22, with tensor pipe at 48.37%. Tensor throughput is the guardrail to protect. The dominant lever for the next round is therefore to unwind register pressure on the PTX 128x128 microkernel surface while keeping the just-earned barrier/short_scoreboard wins, so dir_01 is a narrow register-budget reduction targeting a return to the 168 regs/thread / 3-CTA residency class. dir_02 keeps the same surface but attacks latency hiding via a 3-stage Pg2s/Async Copy pipeline so that with occupancy still constrained we at least spread long_scoreboard across deeper in-flight cp.async traffic. dir_03 reopens the highest-ceiling human-guided 256x128 + 64x64 Right-Left-Right-Left reuse tiling branch as the explore lever that directly matches the persistent tiling guidance but carries the largest rewrite cost. dir_01 is recommended because it is the cheapest direct attack on the measured dominant bottleneck (register pressure driven occupancy collapse) and because the run already shows the rest of the machine state (tensor pipe 48%, compute SM 47.9%) is ready to be fed more warps.

family_audit.reason: Human guidance prefers 256x128 block + 64x64 warp tiling. That matches dir_03 explore, but measured evidence still says the current accepted-base surface is the 128x128 PTX microkernel, so Tiling stays deferred as the exploration lever rather than the primary.
The 8-element cp.async wide loads are already in place (kAsyncCopyElems = 8, 16-byte vector). No stall evidence points at uncoalesced global accesses this round.

The core problem is that each CTA holds 202 registers worth of A/B fragment state. Reworking the 64x64 accumulate microkernel so B fragments (or half the A fragments) are reloaded rather than held live across the full tile is exactly the shared-memory / register-reuse tradeoff dir_01 exploits.
dir_02 keeps leaning on cp.async + wait_group_N, explicitly deepening the pipeline so that long_scoreboard (5.53%) can be hidden without relying on more active warps.

The B tile already uses a +kAsyncCopyElems skew for WMMA. No section evidence this round points to shared-memory bank contention as the dominant loss; keep deferred until a shared-memory-throughput specific hotspot emerges.

L2 throughput is 29.8% and DRAM is only 12.5%. The current kernel already uses grouped-row swizzling (kFixedHotBandPtxGroupedRows = 4). More swizzle tuning is a secondary lever behind register-budget relief.

The primary family for dir_01: the round-10 edit blew up registers/thread by 34 because the restored PTX microkernel holds 16 f32 accumulators x 8 regs plus A/B fragment state. Reworking the 64x64 inner loop ordering (Right-Left-Right-Left across the two 32x32 row pairs) is the cleanest way to cap regs back at the 168 class.

dir_02 is an explicit Pg2s lever: move the 128x128 A/B staging from the current 2-stage double-buffer to a 3-stage pipeline so cp.async latency is hidden against MMA issue when active warps are pinned at 16.58%.

The PTX microkernel already performs shared -> register staging via wmma.load.a/b per column pair. Adding a dedicated Ps2r double-buffer layer would deepen register pressure, which directly conflicts with dir_01. Defer until occupancy is restored.

Multi-buffer beyond 3 stages would require additional shared memory; the round-6 experiment that grew shared memory already measured as a regression. dir_02 caps at 3 stages so Stage remains partially covered but full Stage exploration is deferred.

directions.name: Trim PTX 64x64 Microkernel Register Footprint Back To 3-CTA Class
directions.hypothesis: The round-10 wait-group collapse restored the PTX anchor and fixed the barrier and short_scoreboard stalls, but it caused registers/thread to jump 168 -> 202 because the restored microkernel keeps 16 f32 accumulator fragments plus live A/B fragments across both 64x64 row pairs. By reordering ptx_wmma_accumulate_row_pair_64x64_ptx_microkernel so the two row pairs are interleaved column-by-column (Right-Left-Right-Left across RowPairBase = 0 and 2) instead of sequentially, and by reducing the simultaneous live PtxWmmaBf16Fragment count, we should be able to hold registers/thread below ~180 and restore launch__occupancy_limit_registers = 3. That directly attacks the dominant occupancy_latency_hiding_issue (severity 43.22) without touching barrier wins.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Register-pressure-bound occupancy: 202 regs/thread caps SM residency at 2 CTAs, starving the long_scoreboard / mio_throttle latency hiding budget at 16.58% active warps.
directions.risk: Moderate. The microkernel ordering is performance-sensitive but fully preserves the WMMA math; correctness risk is low and register pressure can be verified with ncu launch__registers_per_thread on the first compile.
ranking_notes: This is the single best upside/risk tradeoff: the dominant bottleneck class (occupancy_latency_hiding_issue, severity 43.22) is directly caused by the 34-register delta produced last round.
If registers fall back to the 168 class, occupancy_limit_registers returns to 3 and we regain most of the latency hiding lost this round, while keeping the barrier stall wins of round 10.
Maps onto the human guidance family Register Reuse (Right-Left-Right-Left warp tile) almost literally, so the direction is also audit-aligned with state/human_guidance.md.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if launch__registers_per_thread does not fall below 190 after the interleave rewrite, or if the rewrite regresses barrier above 8% or runtime above 25.5 ms.
expected_local_changes: Rework ptx_wmma_accumulate_row_pair_64x64_ptx_microkernel so the two row pairs are interleaved column-by-column instead of both sequentially materialized, shrinking the live A-fragment window.
Confirm nvcc does not inflate fragment state by adding --maxrregcount guard only if necessary, avoiding spills to local memory.
Leave wait_group_0 / wait_group_1 ordering from round 10 untouched so recovered barrier and short_scoreboard gains are preserved.

directions.name: Deepen PTX 128x128 Async Copy Pipeline To A 3-Stage Pg2s Buffer
directions.hypothesis: Even if we cannot drop registers immediately, the PTX anchor now pays 5.53% long_scoreboard and 4.0% mio_throttle (both regressed this round). That is a classic symptom of under-hidden global -> shared latency at low occupancy. Moving the double-buffer (2-stage) cp.async scheme to a 3-stage Pg2s ring (so one MMA issue cycle, one cp.async wait_group_2, and one in-flight refill overlap) should cut long_scoreboard and mio_throttle without requiring more active warps, since the hiding now comes from pipeline depth rather than warp count.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Global-to-shared latency hiding at low occupancy: long_scoreboard 5.53 (+3.26 vs prev) and mio_throttle 4.0 (+2.23 vs prev) indicate warps are stalling on cp.async completion instead of making tensor-pipe progress.
code_locations: src/kernels/bf16_gemm_v1.cu:119-132 (FixedHotBandTile128x128: kASharedBytes uses factor 4, which can accommodate 3 stages)
directions.risk: Moderate-high. A 3-stage ring touches the prolog, the steady-state refill order, and the epilogue drain simultaneously; requires careful wait_group_2 / wait_group_1 / wait_group_0 sequencing and 50% more shared memory for A/B buffers.
ranking_notes: Directly targets the two regressed stall classes (long_scoreboard, mio_throttle) without relying on occupancy recovery.
Maps onto the Pg2s, Async Copy, and Stage families from human guidance in one edit.
Ranked second because it grows shared memory and is more fragile than the register trim in dir_01; also, if dir_01 succeeds, much of the latency hiding gap closes for free.
directions.stop_condition: Stop if long_scoreboard does not drop below 4% or if shared memory allocation pushes occupancy_limit_shared_mem below the current occupancy_limit_registers class.
expected_local_changes: Grow the a_shared / b_shared arrays from [2] to [3] stages (already allowed by the factor-4 budget in FixedHotBandTile128x128).
Replace the prolog two cp.async_commit_group calls + wait_group_1 with a three-commit prolog + wait_group_2 schedule.
In the steady-state loop, keep the cp.async_wait_group_0 / __syncthreads() sequence but fire refills two tiles ahead of current stage so three commits are always in flight.

directions.name: Reopen 256x128 Half-Panel Register-Reuse Tiling Branch
directions.hypothesis: The persistent human guidance explicitly calls for 256x128 block tiling with 64x64 warp tiling and a Right-Left-Right-Left warp-tile traversal. FixedHotBandTile256x128 already exists in the repo (kWarpMmaTilesM = 4, kWarpMmaTilesN = 4, 8 warps/CTA) and previous rounds closed the pivot family due to correctness/regression cost, but the current state (occupancy pinned at 2 CTAs/SM anyway) makes a larger tile attractive because it amortizes the A-panel load and the per-warp register accumulator over 4x the math. This is the highest-ceiling branch if dir_01 and dir_02 fail to close the 6.7 ms remaining gap to the 18 ms target.
directions.expected_bottleneck: Tile-granularity / register-reuse ceiling: at 128x128 the A panel is reloaded for every 128 columns; a 256x128 panel doubles A reuse per cp.async batch while keeping B staging compact.
directions.risk: High. This is a broader structural rewrite. Two prior rounds closed 256x128 pivot attempts on correctness/regression grounds; reopening requires re-verifying writer-ownership and half-panel export mapping, and the correctness-first reopens historically regressed to the 28-30 ms class.
metrics_to_recheck: correctness on all 3 dataset cases before trusting runtime
median runtime versus the 24.195072 ms accepted base
ranking_notes: Kept alive because it is the highest-ceiling branch in the repo and directly maps to the persistent human Tiling and Register Reuse guidance.
Ranked third because prior rounds already measured two negative outcomes on reopen attempts, and the current loop has cheaper, more targeted options (dir_01, dir_02) on the nearer family.
Should only be exploited after dir_01 either succeeds and plateaus or fails cleanly on register pressure.
directions.stop_condition: Stop immediately on any correctness failure or any measurement above the 28 ms class; do not spend more than one round on this family without a new machine-state change.
expected_local_changes: Restore the correctness-safe 256x128 writer ownership path.
Wire the PTX anchor launch to the 256x128 kernel for the pivot hot band while keeping the 64x96 tail and 64x384 row-band paths unchanged.
Apply the Right-Left-Right-Left warp tile traversal on the 4x4 = 16 accumulator tiles so B-fragment reuse increases across the warp tile.

notes: Human guidance review for the current round: registers/thread jumped 34 after the round-10 wait-group collapse, collapsing occupancy to 2 CTAs/SM and regressing long_scoreboard and mio_throttle. The dominant lever is therefore Register Reuse on the existing PTX microkernel (dir_01), which maps the measured evidence onto the Right-Left-Right-Left warp tile guidance. Async Copy / Pg2s / Stage is kept alive on the same surface via dir_02 (3-stage cp.async pipeline) as the latency-hiding complement. The 256x128 Tiling branch remains the high-ceiling future move but is held at rank 3 because two previous reopens regressed; it should only be taken when dir_01 is exhausted or fails.
